enrichment.short_description: Assembly is an employee recognition and engagement platform where teams give peer-to-peer shout-outs, celebrate milestones, and redeem rewards.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Assembly lets teams recognize peers, celebrate milestones, and redeem rewards, with AI-powered engagement insights via Dora.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Assembly is an employee recognition and engagement SaaS platform founded in 2017 and based in Santa Monica, California. It helps HR and people-ops teams build workplace culture through peer-to-peer recognition, automated celebrations of birthdays, work anniversaries, and new hires, and structured awards and challenges tied to company values. Recognition is backed by a points-based rewards system that employees can redeem through a global catalog, including gift cards and charitable donations. Assembly also includes 'Dora,' an AI-powered analytics hub that surfaces engagement trends, culture insights, and nudges for managers, alongside advanced people analytics dashboards for HR leaders. The platform integrates with everyday workplace tools such as Slack, Microsoft Teams, and BambooHR so recognition can happen inside existing workflows. Assembly is sold on a subscription basis, with an 'Empower' plan priced at $3 per user per month (billed annually) covering recognition, celebrations, rewards, and Dora AI insights, and a custom-priced Enterprise plan adding advanced security, compliance, and tailored implementation support. It is aimed at organizations of any size — from startups to large enterprises — with a particular focus on remote, hybrid, and distributed teams that need structured ways to keep employees engaged and connected. Assembly is highly rated on G2 for employee recognition software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Assembly is a cloud-based employee recognition and engagement platform designed to help HR teams build connected, high-performing workplace cultures. It centers on peer-to-peer recognition, letting employees and managers give public shout-outs tied to company values.
Beyond recognition, Assembly automates milestone celebrations, runs value-reinforcing awards and challenges, and lets employees redeem earned points through a global rewards catalog.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core features include peer recognition and shout-outs, automated birthday/anniversary/new-hire celebrations, custom awards and challenge programs, and a points-based rewards catalog redeemable for gift cards or donations.
Assembly also offers Dora, an AI-powered insights hub that flags engagement gaps and trends, plus advanced people analytics and native integrations with Slack, Microsoft Teams, and BambooHR.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Overview
paragraphs: Assembly's published Empower plan costs $3 per user per month when billed yearly and includes recognition, celebrations, rewards, points/budget controls, and the Dora AI hub. An Enterprise tier with custom pricing adds advanced security, compliance, and dedicated implementation support, priced after a sales conversation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Assembly targets HR and people-operations teams at organizations of any size, with particular appeal to companies managing remote or hybrid workforces that want a structured, always-on way to reinforce culture and reduce turnover.

features.title: Peer-to-Peer Recognition
features.description: Employees and managers give public shout-outs and appreciation tied to company values.

features.title: Celebrations & Milestones
features.description: Automated recognition of birthdays, work anniversaries, and new hires.

features.title: Awards & Challenges
features.description: Custom programs that reinforce specific company values and behaviors.

features.title: Global Rewards Catalog
features.description: Employees redeem points for gift cards, merchandise, or charitable donations.

features.title: Dora AI Insights
features.description: AI-powered dashboards that surface engagement trends and manager nudges.

features.title: Workplace Integrations
features.description: Native connections with Slack, Microsoft Teams, and BambooHR.

pros: Highly rated on G2 for employee recognition software
Deep integration with Slack and Microsoft Teams for in-workflow recognition
AI-powered engagement insights via Dora
Flexible, points-based global rewards catalog
cons: Full plan comparison and Enterprise pricing require booking a demo
Per-seat subscription costs can add up for large organizations
Some advanced security and customization features are Enterprise-only

faqs.question: What is Assembly?
faqs.answer: Assembly is an employee recognition and engagement platform that lets teams give peer recognition, celebrate milestones, and redeem points-based rewards.

faqs.question: How much does Assembly cost?
faqs.answer: Assembly's published Empower plan starts at $3 per user per month billed annually; Enterprise pricing is custom and requires contacting sales.

faqs.question: Does Assembly integrate with Slack and Microsoft Teams?
faqs.answer: Yes, Assembly natively integrates with Slack, Microsoft Teams, and BambooHR.

faqs.question: Does Assembly offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Assembly's website does not publicly list a self-serve free trial; prospective customers are directed to book a demo for pricing and access.

use_cases.title: Employee engagement & retention programs
use_cases.description: HR teams use Assembly to run structured recognition and rewards programs that reduce turnover.

use_cases.title: Remote/hybrid culture building
use_cases.description: Distributed teams use Assembly's Slack/Teams integrations to keep recognition visible across locations.

enrichment.short_description: Asterisk is a free, open-source framework for building PBX systems, VoIP gateways, and telephony applications, created in 1999 and maintained by Sangoma.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Asterisk is a free, open-source toolkit for building PBX, VoIP, and telephony applications, maintained by Sangoma since its 1999 launch.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Asterisk is a free, open-source software framework for building telephony applications, originally created in 1999 by Mark Spencer of Digium. Since Digium's acquisition, Asterisk has been maintained as part of Sangoma Technologies' open-source telephony portfolio, alongside the FreePBX graphical management interface. At its core, Asterisk turns a general-purpose computer into a full-featured PBX, supporting call routing, interactive voice response (IVR), voicemail, call recording, and conferencing. It implements a wide range of VoIP and telephony protocols, including SIP and IAX2, letting it interconnect traditional PSTN lines, SIP trunks, and VoIP endpoints. Developers can extend Asterisk through its dialplan scripting and APIs such as AGI, AMI, and ARI, making it a common foundation for custom PBX systems, call centers, and commercial VoIP platforms built by telecom providers and integrators worldwide. Asterisk itself has no license fees or paid tiers — it is distributed under an open-source license and typically self-hosted on Linux servers, though it lacks a built-in graphical interface (most deployments pair it with FreePBX or a similar front end). It is best suited for organizations and developers with telephony or Linux systems expertise who need a highly customizable, cost-free alternative to proprietary PBX hardware and software, rather than turnkey plug-and-play users.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Asterisk is an open-source framework for building PBX systems, VoIP gateways, and other telephony applications. Created in 1999 by Mark Spencer, it is now maintained by Sangoma Technologies, which also develops the FreePBX graphical interface commonly paired with it.
Running on standard server hardware, Asterisk handles call routing, IVR, voicemail, conferencing, and interconnection between the PSTN and VoIP networks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Asterisk supports core PBX functionality, wide VoIP protocol coverage (including SIP and IAX2), IVR and call routing, conferencing, and voicemail/call recording.
It is extensible through a dialplan scripting language and APIs (AGI, AMI, ARI), letting developers integrate it with custom applications, call centers, and third-party systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Asterisk suits IT and telecom teams building custom PBX deployments, as well as VoIP service providers and developers building commercial calling platforms, provided they have the Linux and telephony expertise to configure and maintain a self-hosted system.

features.title: PBX Core Engine
features.description: Turns a server into a full-featured private branch exchange.

features.title: VoIP Protocol Support
features.description: Supports SIP, IAX2, and other protocols for interconnecting VoIP and PSTN endpoints.

features.title: IVR & Call Routing
features.description: Configurable interactive voice response and call-routing logic via the dialplan.

features.title: Conferencing
features.description: Built-in audio conferencing capabilities.

features.title: Voicemail & Call Recording
features.description: Native voicemail boxes and call recording.

features.title: Extensible APIs
features.description: AGI, AMI, and ARI interfaces let developers integrate custom applications.

pros: Free and fully open source with no licensing fees
Highly extensible and customizable for bespoke telephony needs
Large, long-running community and ecosystem (25+ years)
Works with a wide range of SIP trunking providers and telephony hardware
cons: Requires Linux and telephony expertise to install, configure, and secure
No built-in graphical interface (commonly requires FreePBX or similar)
Self-hosting means the operator owns all maintenance and security responsibility

faqs.question: Is Asterisk free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Asterisk is free and open source with no licensing cost.

faqs.question: Who maintains Asterisk?
faqs.answer: Asterisk was created by Mark Spencer of Digium in 1999 and is now maintained by Sangoma Technologies.

faqs.question: Does Asterisk include a graphical interface?
faqs.answer: No, Asterisk is configured via its dialplan and config files by default; most users pair it with FreePBX for a web-based GUI.

faqs.question: What protocols does Asterisk support?
faqs.answer: Asterisk supports SIP, IAX2, and other VoIP and traditional telephony protocols.

use_cases.title: Custom PBX deployment
use_cases.description: Businesses build in-house phone systems tailored to their call-routing needs.

use_cases.title: VoIP platform development
use_cases.description: Providers build commercial calling and conferencing platforms on top of Asterisk.

enrichment.short_description: Astra Security is a continuous penetration testing platform combining AI-powered vulnerability scanning with expert-led pentests for web apps, APIs, and cloud.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Astra Security offers AI-powered continuous penetration testing for web apps, APIs, and cloud infrastructure, backed by certified security experts.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Astra Security, operated by ASTRA IT, Inc. and founded in 2018 by Ananda Krishna and Shikhil Sharma, is a continuous penetration testing platform for web applications, APIs, and cloud infrastructure. It combines an AI-powered DAST (dynamic application security testing) scanner covering over 10,000 vulnerability tests, including the OWASP Top 10 and known CVEs, with manual penetration testing performed by certified security experts (PTaaS). Astra's product line also includes an API Security platform that discovers shadow and undocumented APIs and tests them for vulnerabilities, and a Cloud Vulnerability Scanner that checks for 400+ misconfigurations across AWS, Azure, and GCP. Results and remediation guidance flow into a collaborative dashboard, with integrations for Jira, Slack, and CI/CD pipelines, plus a Trust Center for sharing compliance evidence with customers and auditors. Astra sells its DAST scanner, API security, cloud security, and pentest-as-a-service products as separate subscriptions, priced monthly or annually (with a discount for annual billing), alongside a one-week $7 trial offering full platform access. It is aimed at security-conscious engineering and compliance teams — particularly those pursuing SOC 2, HIPAA, or ISO certifications — and reports serving over 1,000 engineering teams across 70+ countries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Astra Security is a continuous penetration testing platform that replaces one-off annual pentests with ongoing, AI-assisted vulnerability scanning combined with expert-led manual testing. Founded in 2018 and headquartered in Claymont, Delaware, it covers web applications, APIs, and cloud infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Astra's DAST scanner runs over 10,000 vulnerability tests, including OWASP Top 10 checks and known CVEs, with AI-suggested fixes. Its PTaaS offering pairs automated scanning with certified human pentesters and real-time collaboration between testers and developers.

The API Security platform discovers shadow and zombie APIs and scans them for vulnerabilities, while the Cloud Vulnerability Scanner checks for 400+ misconfigurations across AWS, Azure, and GCP. Astra also integrates with Jira, Slack, and CI/CD pipelines, and offers a Trust Center for sharing compliance evidence.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Overview
paragraphs: Astra prices its products separately. The DAST Scanner starts at $69/month (Scanner Lite) up to $499/month (Scanner Agency), with annual billing saving roughly 15%. PTaaS plans run from $1,999/year (Pentest Auto) to $5,999/year (Pentest Expert), API Security from $199-$499/month, and Cloud Security from $99-$199/month. Enterprise tiers across all product lines are custom-priced. A $7 one-week trial gives full platform access.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Astra is built for security-conscious engineering teams and compliance-driven organizations pursuing certifications like SOC 2, HIPAA, or ISO, who need continuous rather than point-in-time vulnerability testing.

features.title: PTaaS (Pentest-as-a-Service)
features.description: Manual penetration testing by certified experts with real-time developer collaboration.

features.title: DAST Vulnerability Scanner
features.description: Automated scanning covering 10,000+ vulnerabilities including OWASP Top 10 and CVEs.

features.description: Discovers shadow and undocumented APIs and scans them for vulnerabilities.

features.title: Cloud Vulnerability Scanner

features.title: Workflow Integrations
features.description: Connects with Jira, Slack, and CI/CD pipelines for remediation tracking.

pros: Combines automated scanning with human-verified penetration testing
Broad coverage across web apps, APIs, and cloud infrastructure
Compliance-oriented reporting for SOC 2, HIPAA, and ISO
Used by over 1,000 engineering teams across 70+ countries
cons: Web, API, cloud, and PTaaS products are priced and purchased separately
Enterprise-grade features require a custom quote
May offer more scope than very small teams need for basic scanning

faqs.question: What does Astra Security do?
faqs.answer: Astra Security provides continuous penetration testing that combines AI-powered vulnerability scanning with expert-led manual pentests for web apps, APIs, and cloud infrastructure.

faqs.question: How much does Astra cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing varies by product: the DAST scanner starts at $69/month, PTaaS plans start at $1,999/year, and API/cloud security products range from $99-$499/month, with custom Enterprise pricing available.

faqs.question: Does Astra offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Astra offers a $7 one-week trial with full platform access and no credit card required.

faqs.question: Does Astra combine automated and manual testing?
faqs.answer: Yes, Astra pairs its automated DAST scanner with manual penetration testing performed by certified security experts.

use_cases.title: Continuous vulnerability management
use_cases.description: Engineering teams run ongoing scans against web apps and APIs instead of relying on annual pentests.

enrichment.short_description: athenaOne is athenahealth's cloud-based EHR, practice management, and revenue cycle platform for ambulatory healthcare practices.
enrichment.seo_title: athenaOne: Cloud EHR & Practice Management Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: athenaOne by athenahealth combines EHR, practice management, medical billing, and patient engagement tools for ambulatory care practices.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: athenaOne is a cloud-based, all-in-one healthcare software platform from athenahealth, a company founded in 1997 by Jonathan Bush and Todd Park and now headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts. The platform combines electronic health records (EHR), practice management, medical billing, and revenue cycle management into a single system for ambulatory (outpatient) medical practices. Core capabilities include clinical documentation and charting, scheduling, claims submission and denial management, prior authorization workflows, and patient engagement tools such as online scheduling and messaging. athenahealth positions athenaOne as an integrated, AI-native platform, layering automation and AI-assisted workflows on top of its long-running network of claims and clinical data across many connected practices, which it uses to help identify billing and coding issues. Pricing for athenaOne is not published on athenahealth's website; the vendor works with prospective customers on a custom quote, and industry sources report that some athenahealth pricing models are tied to a percentage of a practice's collections rather than a flat per-provider fee. athenaOne is aimed at physician practices, clinics, and other ambulatory care organizations that want a single vendor covering clinical, billing, and patient engagement needs rather than stitching together separate EHR and billing systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: athenaOne is athenahealth's integrated cloud platform for ambulatory healthcare practices, combining EHR, practice management, medical billing, and revenue cycle management in one system. athenahealth was founded in 1997 and is now headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform covers clinical documentation and EHR charting, scheduling and practice management, medical billing, claims denial management, and prior authorization, alongside patient engagement tools like online scheduling and messaging.
athenahealth markets athenaOne as an AI-native platform, applying automation across billing and clinical workflows and drawing on data from its network of connected practices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: athenaOne is built for ambulatory medical practices and clinics that want clinical, billing, and patient engagement functionality from a single vendor, along with practice administrators managing revenue cycle operations.

features.description: Claims submission, denial management, and collections support.

features.description: Workflow tools to manage payer prior-authorization requirements.

features.title: Patient Engagement Tools
features.description: Online scheduling and patient communication features.

Cloud-based, reducing on-premise IT infrastructure needs
Backed by a large network of connected practices and claims data
Strong focus on revenue cycle and claims denial management
cons: Pricing is not publicly published and requires a custom quote
Some billing models reportedly tie cost to a percentage of collections
Implementation and onboarding can be involved for smaller practices

faqs.answer: athenaOne is athenahealth's integrated cloud platform combining EHR, practice management, medical billing, and patient engagement tools.

faqs.question: Who makes athenaOne?
faqs.answer: athenaOne is made by athenahealth, founded in 1997 and headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts.

faqs.answer: athenahealth does not publish athenaOne pricing; it provides custom quotes, and some reports indicate certain plans are tied to a percentage of a practice's collections.

faqs.question: Is athenaOne cloud-based?
faqs.answer: Yes, athenaOne is delivered as a cloud-based platform rather than on-premise software.

enrichment.short_description: Atomic CRM is an open-source, customizable CRM toolkit by Marmelab built on React, Supabase, and shadcn/ui, available self-hosted or as a managed subscription.
enrichment.seo_title: Atomic CRM: Open-Source, Customizable CRM Toolkit
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Atomic CRM is an open-source CRM built with React and Supabase by Marmelab, free to self-host or available as a managed subscription from €25/month.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Atomic CRM is an open-source CRM toolkit built by Marmelab, a software studio headquartered in Nancy, France, and introduced in 2024. Rather than offering a rigid, one-size-fits-all CRM, Atomic CRM is designed to be customized by developers to fit a business's specific sales process. It includes standard CRM functionality such as contact and company management, note-taking, data import/export, and a Kanban-style deal pipeline, along with more advanced capabilities like single sign-on via SAML, email-to-note automation, API access, and multilingual support. A distinguishing feature is an 'AI harness for Claude Code' that lets non-technical team members request changes to the CRM using natural-language prompts. Atomic CRM is built on a modern stack — React, TypeScript, shadcn/ui, Tailwind CSS, TanStack Query, and Supabase/PostgreSQL — and can be run entirely self-hosted for free under an open-source license, or used as a managed, Supabase-hosted service. Paid managed tiers start with a Starter plan at €25/month, a Team plan at €50/month, and custom Enterprise pricing, with SOC 2, ISO 27001, and HIPAA compliance options available on managed plans. It targets developers and organizations that want full data ownership and deep customization without vendor lock-in, as an alternative to closed commercial CRMs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Atomic CRM is an open-source CRM toolkit created by Marmelab, a French software studio based in Nancy, and introduced in 2024. It's designed for developers who want to adapt a CRM to their specific business process rather than conform to a rigid off-the-shelf tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Atomic CRM covers contact and company management, notes, data import/export, and a Kanban-style deal pipeline. Advanced capabilities include SAML single sign-on, email-to-note automation, API access, mobile access, and multilingual support.
It also includes an AI harness for Claude Code, letting non-technical users request CRM customizations through natural-language prompts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Overview
paragraphs: Atomic CRM is free to self-host under its open-source license. Marmelab also offers managed hosting with a Starter plan at €25/month, a Team plan at €50/month, and custom Enterprise pricing; all managed plans include SAML SSO, email integration, and API access.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Atomic CRM suits developers and technical teams that want full customization and data ownership, as well as small businesses wanting a managed CRM without long-term vendor lock-in.

features.title: Contact & Company Management
features.description: Organize contacts, companies, and notes in one place.

features.title: Deal Pipeline / Kanban Board
features.description: Visualize and manage sales deals through customizable pipeline stages.

features.title: API Access

features.title: AI Harness for Claude Code

pros: Fully open source with complete source-code transparency
Self-hostable for full data ownership and no vendor lock-in
Modern, actively maintained tech stack (React, Supabase, TypeScript)
AI-assisted customization via Claude Code integration
cons: A newer product (2024) with a smaller ecosystem than established CRMs
Self-hosting requires technical setup and ongoing maintenance
Some enterprise features like SSO are tied to managed/paid plans

faqs.question: Is Atomic CRM free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Atomic CRM is free and open source to self-host; Marmelab also offers paid managed hosting plans starting at €25/month.

faqs.question: Who built Atomic CRM?
faqs.answer: Atomic CRM was built by Marmelab, a software development studio based in Nancy, France.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Atomic CRM?
faqs.answer: Yes, Atomic CRM can be fully self-hosted using its open-source codebase and a Supabase/PostgreSQL backend.

faqs.question: What tech stack does Atomic CRM use?
faqs.answer: It's built with React, TypeScript, shadcn/ui, Tailwind CSS, TanStack Query, and Supabase/PostgreSQL.

use_cases.title: Customizable self-hosted CRM
use_cases.description: Technical teams self-host and modify Atomic CRM to fit their exact sales process.

use_cases.title: Managed CRM without lock-in

enrichment.short_description: Atomic Server is a free, open-source, self-hosted graph database and headless CMS for modeling and sharing Atomic Data in real time, built by Ontola.
enrichment.seo_title: Atomic Server: Open-Source Real-Time Graph Database
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Atomic Server is a free, open-source, self-hosted CMS and graph database for modeling and sharing Atomic Data, built in Rust by Ontola.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Atomic Server is a free, open-source graph database and headless CMS for creating, sharing, and modeling 'Atomic Data,' a modular data specification. The Atomic Data project was started in 2020 by Joep Meindertsma, CEO of Ontola, a Netherlands-based software development firm, and Atomic Server remains developed and maintained as part of Ontola's work with an active open-source community. Written in Rust with a React/TypeScript front end, Atomic Server is designed to be extremely lightweight (around an 8MB download) and fast, with sub-millisecond median response times. It functions as a real-time, event-sourced database: data changes sync instantly via WebSockets, supporting collaborative document editing and group chat, full-text search with fuzzy matching, and a schema-validated, Airtable-like table editor. It supports multiple serialization formats including JSON-LD and RDF/XML, built-in authorization controls, and AI integration via the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for connecting with various AI model providers. Atomic Server is entirely free to use, distributed under the MIT license, and intended to be self-hosted. It targets developers and organizations that want a flexible, customizable, self-hosted alternative to commercial headless CMS and database platforms, particularly those who value real-time collaboration and strict, linked-data-style schema modeling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Atomic Server is a free, open-source graph database and headless CMS for modeling and sharing 'Atomic Data.' The project was started in 2020 by Joep Meindertsma of Ontola, a Netherlands-based software firm, and is written in Rust with a React/TypeScript front end.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Atomic Server offers a real-time, event-sourced database with WebSocket-based sync, full-text fuzzy search, collaborative document editing and group chat, and a schema-validated, Airtable-style table editor.
It supports multiple serialization formats (including JSON-LD and RDF/XML), built-in authorization controls, and AI integration via the Model Context Protocol (MCP).

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Atomic Server is aimed at developers and technical teams who want a lightweight, self-hosted, and highly customizable alternative to commercial headless CMS or database platforms, especially those needing real-time collaboration and structured data modeling.

features.title: Real-Time Graph Database
features.description: Event-sourced data storage with instant sync via WebSockets.

features.title: Full-Text Search
features.description: Fast, fuzzy-matching search across stored data.

features.title: Collaborative Editing & Chat
features.description: Real-time collaborative document editing and group chat features.

features.title: Schema-Validated Table Editor
features.description: Airtable-like table editing with strict schema validation.

features.title: Multiple Serialization Formats
features.description: Supports JSON-LD, RDF/XML, and other linked-data formats.

features.title: MCP-Based AI Integration
features.description: Connects to AI model providers via the Model Context Protocol.

pros: Free and fully open source under the MIT license
Extremely lightweight (~8MB) with sub-millisecond median response times
Flexible, schema-based data modeling built on linked-data principles
Supports real-time multi-user collaboration
cons: Requires self-hosting and technical setup
Smaller community and ecosystem than mainstream CMS platforms
Documentation and feature set are still actively evolving

faqs.question: Is Atomic Server free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Atomic Server is free and open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: Who develops Atomic Server?
faqs.answer: Atomic Server is developed by Ontola, a Netherlands-based software firm led by Joep Meindertsma, with an open-source community.

faqs.question: Can Atomic Server be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Atomic Server is designed to be self-hosted; it has a small footprint of around 8MB.

faqs.question: What is Atomic Data?
faqs.answer: Atomic Data is a modular data specification that Atomic Server implements, used for linking and modeling data across systems.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted headless CMS
use_cases.description: Developers use Atomic Server as a flexible, self-hosted content and data backend.

use_cases.title: Real-time collaborative data modeling
use_cases.description: Teams use Atomic Server's table editor and sync features for shared, structured datasets.

enrichment.short_description: Atsumeru is a free, open-source, self-hosted media server for organizing and reading manga, comics, and light novels across devices.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Atsumeru is a free, open-source, self-hosted server for organizing and reading manga, comics, and light novels, with Windows, Linux, and Android apps.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Atsumeru is a free, open-source, self-hosted media server for managing and reading manga, comics, and light novels. Its name comes from the Japanese word for 'to gather,' reflecting its purpose of consolidating a personal digital library in one place. Users organize their collections into categories and sections, and can edit metadata using the ComicInfo.xml or book_info.json formats. Atsumeru supports a wide range of file formats, including CBZ, CBR, CB7, PDF, ePub, FB2, and DjVu, and provides two-way reading-history synchronization so progress carries over between devices. The project is community-driven and open source, distributed with no licensing cost, and is supported through a public GitHub repository and a Telegram community. It ships with manager applications for Windows and Linux and a dedicated Android client for reading on mobile. Atsumeru is aimed at manga, comics, and light-novel enthusiasts who want to self-host their own library server rather than rely on cloud-based or proprietary reading apps, and who are comfortable running and maintaining self-hosted software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Atsumeru is a free, open-source, self-hosted media server built for organizing and reading manga, comics, and light novels. Users build a personal digital library, organized into categories and sections, that can be accessed and read across multiple devices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Atsumeru supports metadata editing via ComicInfo.xml or book_info.json, and reads a wide range of formats including CBZ, CBR, CB7, PDF, ePub, FB2, and DjVu.
It offers two-way reading-history synchronization across devices, along with dedicated manager applications for Windows and Linux and an Android client for mobile reading.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Atsumeru is built for manga, comics, and light-novel enthusiasts who want to self-host their own reading library and are comfortable running and maintaining open-source, self-hosted software.

features.title: Library Organization
features.description: Organize collections into categories and sections.

features.title: Metadata Editing
features.description: Edit item metadata via ComicInfo.xml or book_info.json.

features.title: Multi-Format Support
features.description: Reads CBZ, CBR, CB7, PDF, ePub, FB2, and DjVu files.

features.title: Cross-Device Reading Sync
features.description: Two-way reading-history synchronization across devices.

pros: Free and fully open source
Broad file format support for manga, comics, and light novels
Cross-device reading-progress synchronization
Active community support via GitHub and Telegram
cons: Requires self-hosting and some technical setup
No official iOS app mentioned on the site
Smaller community than mainstream self-hosted alternatives like Komga or Kavita

faqs.question: Is Atsumeru free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Atsumeru is free and open source with no licensing cost.

faqs.question: What file formats does Atsumeru support?
faqs.answer: Atsumeru supports CBZ, CBR, CB7, PDF, ePub, FB2, and DjVu files.

faqs.question: Is Atsumeru self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Atsumeru is designed to be self-hosted on your own server or Windows/Linux machine.

faqs.question: Does Atsumeru have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, Atsumeru offers a dedicated Android client for reading on mobile devices.

use_cases.title: Personal manga/comics library hosting
use_cases.description: Enthusiasts self-host Atsumeru to organize and read their digital manga and comics collections.

use_cases.title: Home media server for light novels
use_cases.description: Self-hosting hobbyists run Atsumeru alongside other home-lab services to manage reading libraries.

enrichment.short_description: Attio is an AI-native CRM that automates go-to-market workflows with AI agents, real-time data sync, and flexible data modeling for revenue teams.
enrichment.seo_title: Attio: AI-Native CRM for Go-to-Market Teams
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Attio is an AI-native CRM with real-time data sync, automation workflows, and AI agents, used by go-to-market teams to run revenue operations.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Attio is an AI-native CRM founded in 2019 and headquartered in London, United Kingdom, backed by investors including a16z, GV, Redpoint Ventures, and Balderton Capital. It's built around a flexible, real-time data model that automatically syncs contact, company, and deal data from connected email, calendar, and other tools, rather than relying on manual data entry. Attio's 'Universal Context' layer unifies emails, calls, product usage, and other connected-tool data into a single record view, and its AI features include 'Ask Attio' for natural-language search and record creation, automation Workflows, outreach Sequences, Call Intelligence for recording and analyzing meetings, and configurable AI agents for research, lead scoring, and other automated tasks. The platform integrates with over 50 tools, including Slack, Notion, Linear, Apollo, Stripe, and email/calendar providers. Attio is sold on a freemium model: a Free plan supports up to 3 seats, Plus and Pro plans add private lists, call intelligence, sequences, and advanced permissions at per-seat monthly pricing (with an annual-billing discount), and an Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with unlimited objects/teams, SSO, and SCIM. Attio is aimed at sales and revenue operations teams — from early-stage startups to larger go-to-market organizations — that want an automation- and AI-heavy alternative to traditional CRMs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Attio is an AI-native CRM founded in 2019 and headquartered in London. It's built around a flexible, real-time data model that automatically syncs contact, company, and deal data, rather than requiring manual entry, and layers AI agents and automation on top of that data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Attio's 'Ask Attio' feature enables natural-language search and record creation, while Workflows automate revenue processes and Sequences power personalized outreach campaigns. Call Intelligence records and analyzes meetings, and configurable AI agents can handle research, scoring, and other automated tasks.
Its 'Universal Context' layer unifies data from email, calls, product usage, and connected tools into a single view, backed by automatic data enrichment and real-time reporting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Overview
paragraphs: Attio offers a Free plan (up to 3 seats) with real-time contact syncing and data enrichment. Plus (€36/user/month, or €29 billed annually) adds private lists and unlimited seats; Pro (€86/user/month, or €69 billed annually) adds Call Intelligence, Sequences, and advanced permissions. Enterprise pricing is custom and billed annually, adding unlimited objects/teams, SSO, and SCIM.

long_form_blocks.heading: Integrations
paragraphs: Attio integrates with more than 50 tools through its App Store, including Slack, Notion, Linear, Apollo, Stripe, Granola, and email/calendar providers, plus support for AI assistants like Claude and ChatGPT.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Attio targets sales, marketing, and revenue operations teams — from early-stage startups to larger go-to-market organizations — that want automation and AI agents to reduce manual CRM upkeep and headcount needs.

features.title: Ask Attio
features.description: AI-powered natural-language search and record creation.

features.title: Workflows
features.description: Automation for orchestrating revenue processes.

features.title: Sequences
features.description: Personalized, automated outreach campaigns.

features.title: Call Intelligence
features.description: Records and analyzes sales calls and meetings.

features.title: Universal Context
features.description: Unified data layer combining emails, calls, product usage, and connected tools.

features.title: AI Agents
features.description: Configurable agents for research, lead scoring, and custom automation.

features.title: Data Enrichment
features.description: Automatic lead research and ICP scoring.

pros: Deep automation and configurable AI agent capabilities
Real-time data sync and flexible, customizable object modeling
Usable free plan for small teams (up to 3 seats)
Wide integration ecosystem with 50+ connected apps
cons: Pro and Enterprise tiers get expensive on a per-seat basis at scale
Some advanced AI and automation features are limited to paid tiers
Newer entrant than legacy CRMs, so its third-party ecosystem is still maturing

faqs.question: Is Attio free?
faqs.answer: Attio offers a Free plan for up to 3 seats with core CRM and data-sync features; paid Plus, Pro, and Enterprise plans unlock more capacity and features.

faqs.question: What makes Attio 'AI-native'?
faqs.answer: Attio builds AI agents, natural-language search, and automated workflows directly into the CRM, rather than adding AI as a bolt-on feature.

faqs.question: Does Attio integrate with Slack and Notion?
faqs.answer: Yes, Attio integrates with Slack, Notion, and 50+ other tools through its App Store.

faqs.question: Who is Attio for?
faqs.answer: Attio is designed for sales, marketing, and revenue operations teams that want to automate go-to-market workflows.

use_cases.title: Startup GTM & sales process building
use_cases.description: Early-stage teams use Attio's flexible data model to build a CRM around their specific sales process.

use_cases.title: Enterprise revenue operations automation
use_cases.description: Larger sales organizations use Attio's Workflows and AI agents to automate pipeline management at scale.
